input_candidates.truth_value: ASTM grain size number: 0Cu 10.2, 1Cu 9.5, 3Cu 9.6. Grain size distributions are shown in Fig. 2 but exact average grain sizes not provided in text.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Potentiodynamic polarization fitting parameters (Table 2): 0Cu — E_corr = −383 ± 6 mV, E_f = −305 ± 4 mV, E_b = −215 ± 5 mV, ΔE = 90 ± 8 mV, I_pass = 1.69 ± 0.06 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; 1Cu — E_corr = −368 ± 7 mV, E_f = −301 ± 3 mV, E_b = −225 ± 4 mV, ΔE = 76 ± 5 mV, I_pass = 1.45 ± 0.08 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²; 3Cu — E_corr = −368 ± 6 mV, E_f = −306 ± 6 mV, E_b = −245 ± 5 mV, ΔE = 61 ± 6 mV, I_pass = 1.07 ± 0.07 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm².

input_candidates.truth_value: The carbide size distribution for 0Cu, 1Cu, and 3Cu are given in Fig. 4 as histograms; no mean size for 3Cu carbides was reported.
Dislocations near carbides act as anodic sites and initiate pitting corrosion, supported by TEM moiré fringe analysis and SKPFM potential measurements.
Mott-Schottky analysis: donor and acceptor densities increase with Cu content (Table 4), indicating higher sensitivity to pitting nucleation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Weight loss data over 5 days immersion (Fig. 9a): 0Cu final relative weight loss approximately −0.0020 g, 1Cu approximately −0.0030 g, 3Cu approximately −0.0035 g.

input_candidates.truth_value: XPS corrosion product composition (Fig. 16): Mn oxide fraction increases from 47% (0Cu) to 77% (3Cu), Al2O3 decreases from 38.2% to 16.3%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: XPS analysis of corrosion products after 5 days immersion (Fig. 16): 0Cu — Fe₃O₄ 4.0%, Fe₂O₃ 6.4%, FeOOH 4.3%, MnO 26.7%, MnO₂ 20.3%, Al₂O₃ 38.2%; 1Cu — Fe₃O₄ 5.5%, Fe₂O₃ 2.9%, FeOOH 2.9%, MnO 27.7%, MnO₂ 29.5%, Al₂O₃ 28.2%, Cu₂O 0.8%, CuO 1%; 3Cu — Fe₃O₄ 1.4%, Fe₂O₃ 2.3%, FeOOH 1.5%, MnO 38%, MnO₂ 39%, Al₂O₃ 16.3%, Cu₂O 0.7%, CuO 0.7%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Passive film formed at -0.3 V (vs. Ag-AgCl) primarily consists of Fe and Al oxides; 0Cu has higher protective oxide content than 3Cu (Fig. 18).
input_candidates.prediction_value: XPS analysis of stable passive film formed at −0.3 V (Fig. 18): 0Cu passivation film — Fe₃O₄ 4.8%, Fe₂O₃ 7.4%, FeOOH 35.5%, MnO 35.5%, MnO₂ 30%, Al₂O₃ 10.9%, Al 5.5%; 3Cu passivation film — Fe₃O₄ 6.7%, Fe₂O₃ 5.4%, FeOOH 43.5%, MnO 43.5%, MnO₂ 24.6%, Al₂O₃ 8.1%, Al 5.3%, Cu₂O₃ 1.1%, CuO 1%.

input_candidates.truth_value: The corrosion mechanism evolves from carbide-induced dislocation corrosion (0Cu) to synergistic microgalvanic corrosion between Cu-enriched grain boundaries and carbides (3Cu), as summarized in Fig. 19.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The corrosion mechanism shifts from dislocation-induced localized pitting near carbides (0Cu) to synergistic microgalvanic corrosion caused by Cu enrichment at grain boundaries combined with austenite-carbide potential differences (3Cu), resulting in severe grain boundary attack and overall degradation of corrosion resistance.

input_candidates.truth_value: Weight loss immersion test in 3.5% NaCl for 5 days: 0Cu weight loss 0.0020 g/cm2, 1Cu 0.0030 g/cm2, 3Cu 0.0035 g/cm2 (approximate from Fig. 9a).

input_candidates.truth_value: The Cu addition in 1Cu refines carbides but also creates more potential difference sites; excessive Cu (3Cu) hinders C diffusion and leads to Cu accumulation at boundaries, deteriorating corrosion resistance.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Minor discrepancy noted: text states 0Cu corrosion rate as 0.0546 mm/a while Fig. 9b bar chart labels it as 0.055 mm/a; both values are very close and indicate the lowest corrosion rate among the three steels.

candidates.truth_value: The Cu addition in 1Cu refines carbides but also creates more potential difference sites; excessive Cu (3Cu) hinders C diffusion and leads to Cu accumulation at boundaries, deteriorating corrosion resistance.
